title: SEGMENT 9 — Enter 3D bioprinting: the printer that prints biology

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — Enter 3D bioprinting: the printer that prints biology
Now we pivot.
Up until now we've been talking about tissue engineering in its classical form — the Langer-Vacanti recipe of pre-formed scaffolds, cell seeding, bioreactor maturation.
Around 2003, a parallel approach started to emerge.
Researchers, looking at the rapidly developing technology of three-dimensional printing — the kind used in industry to make plastic prototypes — asked an obvious question.
Could you adapt 3D printing to print biological materials?
Could you, instead of seeding cells onto a pre-formed scaffold, print the scaffold and the cells together, layer by layer, with the cells already in place?
The answer, it turned out, was yes.
3D bioprinting was born.
The basic idea of bioprinting is to take a "bio-ink" — a hydrogel containing cells — and deposit it through a fine nozzle onto a substrate, building up a three-dimensional structure layer by layer.
Each layer is typically 100 to 300 micrometers thick.
Each pass of the printhead lays down a path defined by a computer-generated pattern.
The cells in the ink are alive and remain alive through the printing process.
After printing, the deposited material is cross-linked — by UV light, by chemical cues, by temperature change — to lock the structure in place.
What you get out, in principle, is a three-dimensional, multi-material, cell-containing object that has the geometry of whatever organ or tissue you wanted to build.
The analogy is direct.
3D bioprinting is to a tissue what a 3D printer is to a plastic part.

You design the geometry in software — typically converting a medical imaging scan, like a CT or MRI, into a printable model — you slice it into layers, you load your bio-ink, you press print, and the machine deposits the material in the right pattern.
The output is custom-shaped, patient-specific, and contains living cells right where they're supposed to be.
There are four major bioprinting technologies, each with its own strengths and limitations.
Extrusion bioprinting is the most common.
The printer pushes bio-ink through a syringe-like nozzle using either a mechanical piston, a pneumatic pressure system, or a screw.
The ink comes out as a continuous filament, like piping frosting onto a cake.
Extrusion is mechanically simple, can handle very thick and cell-dense inks, and can deposit large volumes quickly.
The resolution is moderate — typically 100 to 500 micrometers — because you're limited by the diameter of the nozzle and the viscosity of the ink.
Most commercial bioprinters use extrusion.
Inkjet bioprinting uses a printhead similar to a desktop inkjet printer.
Tiny droplets of bio-ink — typically nanoliters — are ejected from the nozzle by a piezoelectric or thermal actuator.
Inkjet is fast and has good resolution, around 50 micrometers, but the inks have to be much less viscous than extrusion inks, which limits how cell-dense they can be.
Inkjet is popular for thinner tissues like skin.
Laser-induced forward transfer (LIFT) uses a laser pulse to eject droplets from a film of bio-ink onto a substrate.
LIFT has very high resolution — down to 10 micrometers in some setups — but it's slow, expensive, and limits the volumes you can print.
It's primarily a research tool, not a workhorse technology.
Stereolithography (SLA) and digital light processing (DLP) approaches use light to cure a photoreactive bio-ink layer by layer.
The bio-ink is a liquid that solidifies wherever light hits it.
By projecting a 2D pattern of light onto each layer and then advancing the build plate, you can construct three-dimensional structures with very high resolution — down to single-digit micrometers.
SLA/DLP is fast and high-resolution, but it limits you to photo-curable inks, which constrains the chemistry.

The state of the art today, for most applications, is multi-material extrusion — bioprinters with multiple printheads, each loaded with a different bio-ink, that can deposit different cell types and materials in different parts of the same structure.

You might have one printhead loaded with endothelial cells in a soft hydrogel for blood vessel structures, another loaded with smooth muscle cells in a stiffer hydrogel for vessel walls, another loaded with the parenchymal cell type of your target organ in a tissue-mimicking gel.
The print is choreographed in software.
Each layer involves multiple printheads moving in sequence, depositing their cells where they need to go.
This is a remarkable capability.
It is also, today, dramatically less precise than the biology it's trying to recapitulate, which we'll talk about in the resolution segment.
Cells are about 10 micrometers across.
The voxels of a bioprinter — the smallest "pixels" of deposited material — are typically 100 micrometers or larger.
That's a ten-to-one mismatch.
We're trying to print biology with tools that are coarse compared to the biology.
That gap is one of the main engineering challenges of the field.
But still — the fact that you can print a recognizable cartilage construct, with cells, in a few hours, is itself extraordinary.
Twenty years ago this was the stuff of science fiction.
Today, you can buy a desktop bioprinter for the price of a used car, load it with cells, and start printing tissue.
The technology has democratized in a way that very few areas of biomedical engineering have.
